speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three of an interview with Mr. Semyon Menyuk. OK. Should we continue? Could we go back for just a minute, Mr. Menyuk, and would you tell us how old you were and what the year was when these events you've been describing were happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: That it was in the year 1942. The [? pogrom. ?] And I wasn't old, just a baby. 18, 19 years old, approximately. 18 or 19 years. You know, and the time is-- I don't remember now the months or the date. Who can tell now? It was a time to the fall-- closer to the fall time. That's-- approximately, it can be August or in that time. But it was not cool weather. Because when I jumped, I didn't got so clothes. I was only-- my pants and a short and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, OK. Thank you. We can continue now with where we left off.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. When I came to the first house, I need-- I told you that I need to come to my village. I need to come out through a river. Because we didn't got a bridge. The bridges was in Kolki, but I didn't went through Kolki. I must go through the river. When they come to the house, I was asking the people through the window. At nighttime, I'd knock them. It was a big mistake, my mistake, what I've done. No, I was done. And a lady answer me, I cannot come out. I am afraid. I have small children. And I stay like, I don't know-- I'm only alive or I am dead. I am afraid by myself. And she began to tell to me like that. I'd say, I cannot do nothing. And go-- she's telling me, go to another house. Maybe somebody will come out in nighttime, and they will take-- with the boat-- they will take you on the other side river. I went from that place to another house. I came to another house. It's happened the same. I knocked at the window at nighttime.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have the [INAUDIBLE] in my hand. Everybody got afraid at nighttime. Who-- I am afraid, and they got afraid. And I tell him, where is the-- where I can find a boat to come over the river Styr? He tell me, it's right-- very close. Go and you will find a boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I come to the river and I begin to see the boats, all of them, it's closed-- locked with locks. I try. No. And I see it's become the morning. If the light will happen here, I will know-- in danger that somebody will kill me. I begin to try to break off a boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: For a while, I broke off one boat. And what to do? I have only the pitch fork in my hand. And the water in the river Styr is going very fast. To swim-- I'm not a swimmer, but I can swim across the river. I came again to the house. It was very close. And I was asking, maybe can you give me what you have-- Oar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oar. He said, open my barn, it's a door. And you will find near to the door it's stay. You manage-- how I can look? I am afraid. I don't know what is waiting for me on the barn. I would put my hand-- I don't go in the barn. I put my hand, I cannot find nothing. As I take something-- a clothes-- some-- I don't know what it was. And I was thinking, I have the pitch fork. I will put them-- which will be easier to go with the boat through the river. I done this. Done. I come over the river Styr. And it become the day, become the sun. Little night begin to run its left. Maybe at 3 kilometers. Now, I feel that I will come in time. I was running, and I come to [INAUDIBLE] my village, Komarovo, so I know the people. I come to one guy. I was-- his son. I was playing with his son. We was good buddies.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, he was not at home. I don't know where he was in that time. When I knock him in the door in morning, very early, he asked, who is it? I say, I'm that and that. It's Sem. No. He called me a [? Strudik. ?] He-- Was-- excuse me. Was this a Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Ukraine. No Jewish more.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were none left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish. Yes. And I came to-- he'd give me something to eat. No. Can you eat? I don't want to eat nothing. I ask him what to do. He tell me, I cannot keep you here. If I will keep you in the home and somebody will find out that you're here, they'll kill me and they'll kill you. He tell me a thing like this. What he hear from people in the village. But it's in the forest. The other guy-- his name, the same [? Strudik, ?] last name Feuerman. I know him. He left. He jumped from the forest [? pogrom ?] that I've told you before. He jumped, and he is in forest. Now, he tells me, I don't know exactly the place where he's-- now he tell me the name, a place. But I know where it is approximately. It's called, in Russian, [SPEAKS RUSSIAN]. I know where it is because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that a town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not a town. It's only some houses in forest. But in Poland, a lot of people, they bought the land. And they make the land in the forest. They built a house. And they was living not in the village, outside the village. Now, I decided, let me run. That's-- no, I don't need to go with main-- with main highways. I went through forest, and I came to the place-- what he told me-- [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. I came to a house, too. And I tell them-- they know me, too. And I tell him what I hear from a Ukraine person-- man. He tell me, yes, he's coming here. He comes to ask for his bread or potatoes or something to eat. Now, how to find him. I cannot tell who he comes. No. He don't say where he stay. Now, the same with me. I decided what to do. I went to the forest. He gave me, too-- some piece bread or I don't remember what he-- he give to me something. I came in the forest, you understand-- after things like that. In the heart, it's terrible. My, my, my mind-- I lost my mind at all. I-- night and day, I cannot sleep. I cry. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Now, I feel nobody can help me. And nobody I can help to somebody. I know, that's all. I lost all my family. No parents, no-- how to be-- one day, I was walking in forest. And I see this-- not far away, I see a person in forest. My instinct tell me, like, try. It's not a bad person. Now, in this place, what I was-- a person like himself will not come. It was a big forest. And we decided-- in the same-- and he decided to come each to other. And that's after, maybe, I was walking by myself two weeks like that. So, two weeks, I was by myself. Now, we come together. I recognize him. He recognized me. And we begin to talk. I told him how I jumped. He told me how he jumped. His situation was a little different from my situation. Now, it's very close. He run when they put him in the synogogue-- in the forced [? pogrom ?] that I told you. They put them in synagogues. He-- something I don't know how. He take off through a window in the synagogue. It was near to the river Styr. And he was a good swimmer in the water. He swimmed under the water in the other side river. And he stayed until night. And at nighttime, he came to this village. He explained to me how he-- no, now together, the two of us-- we can say a word each to other. The help is the same now. It's a little easier, but we can talk. It's easier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, that's begin our life-- what to do, how to be [? that. ?] Again, 1943, it's beginned to go the time to fall and become more cool. What we eat? Believe me-- how many days we was, hungry but we didn't eat nothing. We didn't got water. Water, we drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: We come the forest and we push with the feet-- the foot-- and it's come out when it's-- the water-- the underground water come up. And we take the water to drink. But who knows what is in the water. Now, the food the same as when it was summertime, it was easier. Where we steal, where we was asking something for food. In that time, every day, every week-- every week, it was worse, worse for us. Because the Germans begin-- the police was telling, if they will find something about who keep some-- a Jew or give food or what, we will kill them and them. Some people stopped to give us some food. They didn't stop to give it to all. It was times what we came at nighttime to people. They make potatoes for the pigs. That's not-- they didn't take off the skin. With the ground, for pigs. If they give us, we stay with him and we eat, maybe, a half box or-- that's the potatoes. You know, the stomach becomes like this. Doesn't-- it's terrible and hungry. And in not ten minutes, you are again hungry. We ate some [? flour ?] with water. I mix-- we ate like this. We didn't got no-- now, it's episodes to tell us every day's episodes. And behind this, what you don't have enough to eat in that situation, morality is-- you're dead-- you know what somebody can kill you, somebody can-- something happen that-- and how it's bad, this life. How it's terrible. How a person want to live. And to make, maybe-- sometimes, I remind [? myself ?] how many times he was talking with him. Now, it become worse and worse. I tell you like-- in that time that we was in the forest-- what we went-- we didn't got-- it was a lot of lice. Excuse me, please. A lot of lice. We didn't-- the skin-- you cannot find one place what is without blood. All the body is in blood. And it's terrible, but you have-- it's unbearable. Sometimes, I remember now, when we come sometimes to people at nighttime to ask for a piece of bread or to eat or what. And he come in a barren place what it's warm. Believe me, you cannot stay in a-- like, some things-- something your body is shaking with so much lice in the body. Now, I told you what. It's become worse with time-- worse and worse it become. The full time begun-- the first time. [COUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Winter time, what to do? What we can do? One guy give us advice. You know what? Make from wood, and put hay-- put something to make them more barren. The forest froze.

speaker: interviewee
text: The cool come-- that from 1942-- the winter time-- from 1942 to 1943. We made this impossible to stay. The forest night, you cannot stay. We don't have the clothes. I got a clothes-- only pants. And he was asking somebody from the Ukraine-- from the people-- they give us a jacket. No, it's not warm. It's cool. It's winter time. It's Russia, frozen in snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: One guy-- the other guy-- tell us, you know what to do to save your body? Take-- dig in the ground. We begin to do this.

speaker: interviewee
text: We take-- one guy, he give us a shovel. And we begin to make the place to stay in the ground. Now, what to do. We need to make the ground, but nobody can see that's what-- we take the ground and we put them in forests. Places where nobody can see what somebody was doing a job to take a ground. And we take some-- all we was working night-- the daytime, we didn't work-- at nighttime. We was worked-- what nobody can see. We've got afraid that the day time-- we make the hole. We put in the cover. Cover to cover, it was pieces wood. Then we make a hole to come inside. We come inside and we put [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] like small tree is standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- what nobody can see what it's inside. And we done. Yes, in the ground, it was better. We cannot frozen. We feel better.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't mention one things. But it's happened that when one night, when we came to one person that's-- he was in the village. And we came to him. We want to know news, something. Can you tell us the news? What-- where is it now? What's he write in the newspapers? We was very interested in how long it will take us to stay. One day, he tell us-- you know what? He tell us [? Thrudiky, ?] because his name's [? Thrudiky, ?] my name's [? Thrudiky. ?] Only difference in the last names. He was, today, in the church. And people told, today at nighttime is coming the police. And they will check for us to kill us. You know what? Don't go in this place Zabolot. When he told us that, we didn't went to Zabolot, in this area, maybe, for three weeks, two weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: After two weeks, we came in this same place. And they told us-- the people were delivered. We came off to [INAUDIBLE] to-- Each night, we came to somebody. We changed only the schedule. If we come today to one, we don't go the other night to the same. We go to another one until it's past a week, and we came again. He tell us-- we was thinking what they will kill us. Will kill you. Police came and stayed, maybe, three nights. They was standing and waiting for us. But we will come and they will kill us. Imagine what time-- how big it was, the anti-Semitism. How big it was, the-- to kill us. It's come to two people in the forest-- two Jewish. And we jumped from the truck. And so many people-- they didn't-- and we didn't come. And we-- then we were sure what he told us. The right time is that what we now decided what he's the best man. Not everybody can tell us to help us. Now, we was coming often to him, and he helped us how he can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Comes-- become winter time. The cold winter time, terrible. Was this the winter of '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. '43. '42, '43, the winter time. No clothes. Fire, we can make only at day time. No nighttime fire because you can see at nighttime the fire. Matches. We didn't got matches. If somebody give us a match, we made them divided. Maybe, for four pieces, to use them, one match. And most of them, we was using-- no, that was maybe before peopled civilization. But it was a piece stone and a piece metal. A guy would give us. And we make for there's the fire. And if we make the fire at day time, we need to make them what it will be dry wood. You cannot make them [INAUDIBLE] smoke, what somebody can see. We take off our clothes. Winter time at all because then we go to ask for something to eat, we don't with the highways. We go in the ways what we need to go through water. And we come wet. You cannot come in the house anywhere to dry up your clothes. Because only everything is in forest. Everything in the forest. And we was-- from that time, we was so smoked in our clothes. But when we come in the house-- after that, when they come after the war-- they told us what the house was stinky. Maybe for a week, was stinky the house. From that's what we came in the house with the smoke. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] when we stay in the forest, in a while, one guy tell us, in the next village is one Jew. And the Jew is related to that person, but we eat together. With that's [? Strulik ?] Feuerman, his sister, his sister's husband. We was talking with some people-- they help him. And they brought him to us. No one keep-- they brought him. He came to us. It was frozen. All the foot, the fingers. No fingers at all, and both feet. He cannot walk. Now, then we begin-- I and he and that, the third one, his brother-in-law. He was standing-- we went, and his name is Stupak Motel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We went to ask something food for us, and we bring for him something. Now, hungry, always hungry. Now, we was-- when you see us in that time, you will never see what it's people. It's like animals. We didn't shave. We didn't cut our hair. Everything is dark. The hand-- you cannot see some clear places on the hand, on the face. Now, all the winter time we was in the hole in the ground. Then, in spring, when the water come out-- the underground water-- become up and up and we cannot stay more. Then we get out from the place, from the holes. And we begin-- oh, it's become more-- the climate, it was better. No frost-- frozen more. And we stay in forest. Never we was anywhere asleep. You find places if you know how it's going. Winter time, the people in the villages, they make hay for the animals. In summer time, you cannot come in the places because it's a lot of water. You cannot take the leave that's on the ground and in the forest. Then, only winter time, they pick up these. We was going into sleep, sleep-- how-- it will be but sometime, you want to sleep. We came to the-- where is the hay. We come inside and we stay for a night. In morning, we get up and run in another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times we was be going in for a night? Maybe we make 10, 15 kilometers from one side to another side to find something to eat. And we-- tired-- with no clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stay in the ground. The snow-- we clean up the snow and put a little-- from the woods-- leaves or what. And we put-- he take off his jacket, and I take off his jacket, and we stay back to back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in this position, we were sleeping, maybe-- how in the frost, how in the cool, how you can sleep? How it will be? No, maybe 10 minutes, 15 minutes, you're tired and you sleep. And then, we get up and run again. Was Mr. Stupak with you through all of this? Was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He-- that's what I-- they brought him from the other side. He stay-- if you bring the food and he stay in one place. Then, when we come out from the ground, he come out, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he manage to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't move at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to carry him, or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't move from the place. He was staying in one place for that when we make the hole, he was standing. Then, when it was the water, we make him another place--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in forest, not everywhere. And we was asking some people to make us some medicines. You know? With no medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
